ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03311069); no specific entry for wildflower honey milk tea exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific honey milk tea sub-code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for honey milk tea or Taiwanese milk tea. None assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium-sized cup of approximately 500 ml, as typically served at KOI Thé and similar Taiwanese milk tea chains in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Milk tea, prepared from tea and whole milk with added sugar — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with USDA honey entry and standard black tea composition for wildflower honey milk tea estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for wildflower honey milk tea exists in any approved primary source. Values estimated by component composition: brewed black tea (~98% water, negligible macros), whole milk (USDA FDC #01077: protein 3.2 g, fat 3.3 g, carbohydrate 4.8 g per 100 g), and wildflower honey (USDA FDC #169640: carbohydrate ~82 g, sugar ~82 g per 100 g). A typical medium KOI-style honey milk tea (500 ml) is estimated to contain approximately 40% milk, 55% brewed tea, and 5% honey by volume/weight, yielding per-100 g values as documented. Energy calculated using Atwater factors (4/4/9 kcal per g protein/carbohydrate/fat).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of milk tea estimated from analogous components: whole milk (GI ~27, Atkinson et al. 2008) and honey (GI ~55, Foster-Powell et al. 2002, International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC. International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002;76(1):5-56.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for wildflower honey milk tea. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the dominant glycemic contributor being wildflower honey (GI ~55 per University of Sydney GI Database and Foster-Powell et al. 2002 international tables) blended with whole milk (GI ~27). The weighted blend of honey and milk in this beverage yields an estimated composite GI of approximately 55. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 42.5) / 100 = 23.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~90 mg per 500 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 18 mg sodium per 100 g (90 mg per medium serving), wildflower honey milk tea poses minimal sodium concern for hypertensive individuals; however, regular consumption of sweetened beverages should be moderated as part of an overall dietary pattern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content from wildflower honey (~41 g sugar per 500 ml serving) with low glycemic index (55) and high glycemic load (~23 g per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid wildflower honey milk tea due to its high free sugar load (~41 g per medium serving) and low GI of 55, which can still contribute to significant postprandial blood glucose elevation given the high carbohydrate load; if consumed, portion size should be strictly limited and paired with low-GI foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from milk (~4 g per 500 ml serving) and high free sugar content contributing to triglyceride risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of honey milk tea contributes moderate saturated fat (~4 g per serving) and high free sugars (~41 g per serving), both of which are associated with elevated triglycerides and LDL cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to occasional consumption and choose lower-sugar formulations where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wildflower Honey Milk Tea (Medium) — known in Chinese as 野花蜜奶茶 (Yě Huā Mì Nǎi Chá) — is a Taiwanese-style sweetened milk tea beverage sold at KOI Thé and similar specialty tea chains in Singapore. It is prepared by brewing high-quality black tea, blending it with fresh or reconstituted whole milk, and sweetening it with wildflower honey, which imparts a distinctive floral sweetness distinct from refined sugar or standard honey. The beverage is typically served cold over ice in a medium cup of approximately 500 ml. The texture is smooth and creamy with a light tea astringency balanced by the natural sweetness of wildflower honey. Nutritionally, a medium serving provides approximately 260 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 6.5 g fat, and 42.5 g carbohydrate, of which approximately 41 g is free sugar derived primarily from the honey. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low) and the glycemic load per serving is approximately 23 g, making it a high-glycemic-load beverage. Clinically, this drink is of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its high free sugar content and significant glycemic load per serving. It also contributes moderate saturated fat and should be consumed in moderation by those with cardiovascular disease. Sodium content is low, posing minimal concern for hypertensive individuals. The beverage is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains dairy and is not suitable for vegans or those with lactose intolerance. It is a popular afternoon or social beverage in Singapore's Taiwanese tea culture.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for wildflower honey milk tea exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are estimated from component composition using USDA SR Legacy data for whole milk (FDC #01077) and wildflower honey (FDC #169640) with standard brewed black tea composition. The estimation methodology is transparent and defensible but introduces uncertainty in exact macro values. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for honey and milk. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates: approximately 40% whole milk, 55% brewed black tea, and 5% wildflower honey by weight in a typical medium serving. Exact formulation varies by outlet and customisation (sugar level, ice level, milk type). 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero by formulation; whole milk contains negligible naturally-occurring trans fats below reporting threshold, treated as 0.0 for this record. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — naturally zero; tea, milk, and honey contain no dietary fibre. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this beverage type in the source database and varies by milk fortification status. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 7. GI set to 55 (low) based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database honey entry (GI ~55) and whole milk (GI ~27); composite weighted estimate. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (55 × 42.5) / 100 = 23.4 g. 8. This record covers the medium (M) size as sold at KOI Thé Singapore; large and small sizes will have proportionally different serving weights and total nutrient loads. 9. Halal status based on standard KOI Thé Singapore halal certification; individual outlet certification should be verified. 10. Price SGD 6.20 preserved from field survey data source record.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed with component-based nutrient estimation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI set to 55 (medium) via analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database.

Iteration 2: Addressed validator feedback for q4_glycemic_completeness. Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for glycemic_index 55, as per scoring model rules (GI <= 55 is low). Updated gi_source.source_match_notes, health_context.type2_diabetes key_factor and guidance, and general notes to reflect the 'low' GI category. Verified aliases_by_language consistency and documentation of -1/0.0 sentinel values. All hard gates and Q1–Q8 confirmed passing.
